addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Logs writer (for Cloud Build logs to Cloud Logging)
  rbgi_add_project_iam_role "${z_token}" "Grant Mason Logs Writer" "projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}" \
    "roles/logging.logWriter" "serviceAccount:${z_mason_sa_email}" "mason-logs-writer"

  buc_step 'Provision Cloud Build service agent'
  # The Cloud Build Service Agent (service-{PN}@gcp-sa-cloudbuild.iam.gserviceaccount.com)
  # needs serviceAccountTokenCreator on Mason to impersonate it during builds.create.
  # The service agent is auto-created when the Cloud Build API is enabled; its email is
  # deterministic from the project number. See RBSCIP for the two-SA distinction.
  rbgu_provision_service_agent "cloudbuild" "${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}" "${z_token}" > /dev/null \
    || buc_die "Failed to provision Cloud Build service agent"
  local -r z_cb_service_agent="service-${z_project_number}@gcp-sa-cloudbuild.${RBGC_SA_EMAIL_DOMAIN}"
  buc_log_args "CB service agent: ${z_cb_service_agent}"

  buc_step 'Enable Cloud Build service agent to impersonate Mason'
  rbgi_add_sa_iam_role "${z_token}" "${z_mason_sa_email}" "${z_cb_service_agent}" "roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator"

  buc_step 'Submit per-pool probe builds (materialize quota rows + assert egress posture + GAR write)'

  # Tether posture: public reachable AND Google reachable.
  local -r z_tether_egress_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_probe_tether_egress.sh"
  printf '%s\n' \
    '#!/bin/bash' \
    'set -euo pipefail' \
    'curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://example.com > /dev/null' \
    'echo "tether: public reachable OK"' \
    'curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com > /dev/null' \
    'echo "tether: google reachable OK"' \
    > "${z_tether_egress_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to write tether egress script"

  # Airgap posture: public BLOCKED AND Google reachable. Public-reachable is
  # a hard infra-drift signal — curl's connection error surfaces in the build
  # log as the assertion fails.
  local -r z_airgap_egress_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_probe_airgap_egress.sh"
  printf '%s\n' \
    '#!/bin/bash' \
    'set -euo pipefail' \
    'if curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://example.com > /dev/null; then' \
    '  echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2' \
    '  exit 1' \
    'fi' \
    'echo "airgap: public blocked OK"' \
    'curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com > /dev/null' \
    'echo "airgap: google reachable OK"' \
    > "${z_airgap_egress_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to write airgap egress script"

  zrbgp_pool_probe_submit "${z_token}" "tether" "${z_tether_id}" "${z_mason_sa_email}" "${z_tether_egress_file}"
  zrbgp_pool_probe_submit "${z_token}" "airgap" "${z_airgap_id}" "${z_mason_sa_email}" "${z_airgap_egress_file}"

  buc_step 'Update depot tracking'
  zrbgp_depot_list_update || buc_die "Failed to update depot tracking after creation"

  buc_success 'Depot creation successful'
  buc_info "Next: mantle Governor for this depot:"
  buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_MANTLE_GOVERNOR}"
}

I have the mechanism. Pausing to report before doing anything else, per mid-execution posture.

**Failure**: `rbtdrp_depot_stand_up` in pristine-lifecycle fixture. `depot levy` exits 1 at the new "Submit per-pool probe builds" step (added in ₢BOAAD this morning) with: `Probe submission failed for tether: workerpool not found: "projects/prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100011/locations/us-central1/workerPools/prlcbhl-pristlbhl100011-pool-tether"`.

**Mechanism**: `workerPools.create` is a Cloud Build Long-Running Operation. The HTTP 200/201 at `rbgp_Payor.sh:904` only acknowledges receipt — the response body is an `Operation`, not a ready pool. The new probe submission at `rbgp_Payor.sh:1098-1099` fires `builds.create` against the pool by resource name *immediately* after the create call, before provisioning has completed, and `builds.create` rejects with "workerpool not found." The prior levy worked because nothing else in the script touched the pool — first real use was later (hallmark conjure), by which point it had become ready on wall-clock time.

The `Verify IAM propagation before resource creation` step at line 945 polls the Artifact Registry repositories endpoint, which doesn't gate on pool readiness.

**Specific repair I'd attempt**: between pool creation (line 937) and probe submission (line 1098), poll each pool's Operation to terminal state. Capture the operation name from the create response's `.name` field, then `rbgu_poll_until_ok` against `${RBGC_API_ROOT_CLOUDBUILD}${RBGC_CLOUDBUILD_V1}/${operation_name}` until the response shows `.done == true`. (Or poll `workerPools.get` until 200 — simpler, but the Operation handle is the canonical signal.) Do this for both tether and airgap pools before either probe fires.

Want me to proceed with the repair, or do you want to shape the docket/segment this first?

1	==== Decision
2	
3	IAM propagation delays for Cloud Build v2 connection creation are handled by
4	connection-creation retry with exponential backoff, not by an impersonation
5	readiness gate or fixed sleep delays.
6	
7	Three propagation classes are recognized by the IAM grant harness in
8	`zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate`, all sharing the same exponential-backoff
9	budget defined by the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` constants:
10	
11	* **Class A — forward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
12	  calls fail with HTTP 400 "does not exist" when a newly-created service
13	  account is not yet visible to the IAM policy service as a valid member.
14	* **Class B — backward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
15	  calls fail with HTTP 400 "is not deleted" when a recently-deleted service
16	  account is still referenced in existing policy bindings.
17	* **Class C — caller-recently-empowered**: `getIamPolicy` / `setIamPolicy`
18	  on a resource-scope target (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) returns HTTP 403
19	  because the caller's recently-granted role (e.g. fresh `roles/owner` on a
20	  newly-levied depot) has not yet reached the resource-scope IAM cache,
21	  even though it has reached project- and SA-scope caches.
22	
23	Class C is time-bounded only — no body fingerprinting. The bound itself is
24	the discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial
25	waits the budget and fails cleanly. The same model is already proven in
26	{rbtgo_access_jwt_probe}. Resource-scope grant sites declare 403 tolerance
27	with an empty body-glob; project-scope sites do not.
28	
29	* **Secret access readiness**: The CB v2 connection creation LRO itself serves as the propagation test.
30	  If the Cloud Build Service Agent cannot yet access the secrets (IAM grant not propagated), the LRO fails.
31	  The caller retries with exponential backoff, deleting the zombie connection resource between attempts.
32	* **Secret resource propagation**: `getIamPolicy` on a newly created secret may return HTTP 404 while
33	  the Secret Manager resource propagates across Google's control plane.  The API documents that
34	  `getIamPolicy` returns an empty policy (HTTP 200) when no policy has been set — so 404 indicates
35	  the resource itself is not yet visible, not that the policy is absent.  After creating secrets,
36	  `rbgu_poll_until_ok` polls each secret's `getIamPolicy` endpoint until 200 before proceeding to
37	  grant IAM bindings.
38	
39	* **SA keys subresource propagation**: After
40	  link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/create[serviceAccounts.create]
41	  returns 200, the keys subresource read path
42	  (link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/list[serviceAccounts.keys.list])
43	  can lag the SA email path independently — empirically observed as a non-monotonic 200→200→404 flap where
44	  both the SA GET probe and a keys subresource `rbgu_poll_until_ok` probe return 200 while the real
45	  `keys.list` call immediately returns 404 on the same URL.  Mitigation: retry the actual `keys.list`
46	  call on HTTP 404 using the same backoff budget as step 7 keys.create (`RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_*`) —
47	  same underlying physics (subresource visibility after parent creation).  A separate
48	  `rbgu_poll_until_ok` proxy probe before `keys.list` was found insufficient: it returned 200 in the
49	  same flap window that the real call observed 404, exemplifying why proxy probes are not retained as
50	  correctness gates (see "What Dies" below).  See evidence row below.
51	
52	==== Rationale
53	
54	The connection creation LRO tests the actual operation, not a proxy.
55	When the Cloud Build Service Agent accesses resources requiring recently-granted permissions,
56	a successful LRO proves end-to-end readiness: the IAM grant has propagated,
57	the service agent identity is active, and the resource content is valid.
58	
59	Fixed `sleep` delays ("sleep and pray") are unreliable:
60	
61	* Google's IAM propagation documentation states changes "typically take less than 2 minutes"
62	  but provides no guaranteed upper bound.
63	* A 15-second sleep is too short for worst-case propagation and wastefully long for the common case.
64	* Retry-on-error tests the actual operation: if the grant has propagated, the retry succeeds immediately;
65	  if not, the retry continues until the deadline.
66	
67	The `testIamPermissions` API cannot serve as a readiness gate because it only tests the caller's own
68	permissions on a resource -- it cannot test whether a third-party service account (like the CB service agent)
69	has been granted access to a secret.
70	
71	==== Superseded: Impersonation Readiness Gate
72	
73	The prior approach used an impersonation readiness gate: mint a short-lived token as the CB service agent
74	via `generateAccessToken`, then call `accessSecretVersion` with that impersonated token, retrying on 403
75	until the grant propagates.  This approach was abandoned due to three problems discovered 2026-03-03:
76	
77	. **roles/owner does NOT grant iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken** --
78	  The explicit `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant (previously deleted as unnecessary per
79	  an earlier RBSCIP revision) was in fact required for impersonation.  Without it,
80	  `generateAccessToken` returns persistent 403.  roles/owner is a broad role but does not include
81	  the `iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken` permission needed for impersonation.
82	
83	. **Wrong SA identity** -- The code impersonated the default build SA
84	  (`{PN}@cloudbuild.gserviceaccount.com`) but CB v2 connections use the Cloud Build Service Agent
85	  (`service-{PN}@gcp-sa-cloudbuild.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).  Even if impersonation worked,
86	  it would have tested the wrong principal.
87	
88	. **No non-impersonation alternative exists** -- `testIamPermissions` and IAM Policy Analyzer
89	  reflect policy intent (what the policy document says), not enforcement state (whether the grant
90	  has actually propagated).  Only actual access as the principal proves propagation.
91	
92	Pattern B (connection-creation retry) eliminates `generateAccessToken`, `accessSecretVersion`,
93	and any need for `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grants for the readiness gate.
94	
95	==== Evidence
96	
97	[cols="1,3",options="header"]
98	|===
99	|Claim
100	|Source
101	
102	|IAM changes propagate eventually, typically < 2 minutes
103	|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/access-change-propagation[Access change propagation] -- "After you grant access, the access change takes effect after a period of time known as propagation delay"
104	
105	|Newly created SA may not be immediately usable
106	|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-create[Creating service accounts] -- "After you create a service account, you might need to wait 60 seconds or more before you use the service account"
107	
108	|testIamPermissions tests caller permissions only
109	|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/testing-permissions[Testing permissions] -- tests whether the caller has the specified permissions on a resource
110	
111	|CB v2 connection creation uses the Cloud Build Service Agent to read secrets
112	|https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/github/connect-repo-github[Connect a GitHub host] -- the Cloud Build Service Agent must have `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` on the secrets used by the connection
113	
114	|SA keys subresource read-path can flap non-monotonically (200→200→404) after SA create
115	|`Memos/memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` -- transcript-level evidence, pristine depot `prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009`, 2026-05-14
116	|===
117	
118	==== What Dies
119	
120	* The impersonation readiness gate (`generateAccessToken` + `accessSecretVersion` as a pre-check)
121	* Any `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant solely for the readiness gate
122	  (build-time Mason impersonation grants are unrelated and preserved)
123	* Fixed `sleep 15` delays before CB v2 connection creation and after Governor SA creation
124	* Standalone `rbgu_poll_until_ok` as a correctness gate for IAM propagation
125	  (the SA GET poll remains as a fast-path existence check, not a grant propagation gate)
126	* Any assumption that SA existence implies SA usability in IAM policy operations
127	
128	==== What Survives
129	
130	* SA GET poll via `rbgu_poll_until_ok` -- retained as a fast-path check that the SA resource itself
131	  is readable, which typically resolves well before IAM policy propagation completes
132	* The existing 429/5xx transient retry loop in {rbtoe_iam_grant_project} step 3 --
133	  this handles a different failure class (rate limiting, server errors) and is preserved inside
134	  the new outer propagation retry
135	* All IAM grant functions ({rbtoe_iam_grant_project}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
136	  {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}) -- enhanced with propagation retry,
137	  not replaced
138	* Cloud Build service agent `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant on Mason SA --
139	  this is for build-time impersonation (Cloud Build executing as Mason), unrelated to the readiness gate
140	
141	==== Backoff Profile
142	
143	All IAM-grant propagation retries — every site in `rbgi_IAM.sh` plus the
144	inline GAR retry in `rbgg_invest_director` — use the same single profile
145	locked in `rbgc_Constants.sh`:
146	
147	* Initial delay: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC` (3 seconds)
148	* Multiplier: 2x (per-site arithmetic, not a kindle constant)
149	* Cap: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC` (20 seconds)
150	* Deadline: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC` (420 seconds; 7 minutes)
151	
152	This profile accommodates Google's documented "typically < 2 minutes" with margin for worst-case
153	propagation while avoiding indefinite waits. It is the single budget shared
154	across all three propagation classes (A: forward member-visibility, B:
155	backward member-visibility, C: caller-recently-empowered); operator value
156	changes propagate to every grant site by editing the constants once. The
157	JWT-bearer consumer-side retry uses its own budget
158	(`RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_*`) — different cost-of-waiting profile.
159	
160	==== References
161	
162	* Research conducted: 2026-03-03 (API documentation review, live 403 diagnosis)
163	* Google IAM documentation consistently describes eventual consistency without hard SLA bounds
164	* Pattern B (connection-creation-as-probe) replaces the original `generateAccessToken` / `accessSecretVersion` gate
165

==== Decision

IAM propagation delays for Cloud Build v2 connection creation are handled by
connection-creation retry with exponential backoff, not by an impersonation
readiness gate or fixed sleep delays.

Three propagation classes are recognized by the IAM grant harness in
`zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate`, all sharing the same exponential-backoff
budget defined by the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` constants:

* **Class A — forward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
  calls fail with HTTP 400 "does not exist" when a newly-created service
  account is not yet visible to the IAM policy service as a valid member.
* **Class B — backward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
  calls fail with HTTP 400 "is not deleted" when a recently-deleted service
  account is still referenced in existing policy bindings.
* **Class C — caller-recently-empowered**: `getIamPolicy` / `setIamPolicy`
  on a resource-scope target (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) returns HTTP 403
  because the caller's recently-granted role (e.g. fresh `roles/owner` on a
  newly-levied depot) has not yet reached the resource-scope IAM cache,
  even though it has reached project- and SA-scope caches.

Class C is time-bounded only — no body fingerprinting. The bound itself is
the discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial
waits the budget and fails cleanly. The same model is already proven in
{rbtgo_access_jwt_probe}. Resource-scope grant sites declare 403 tolerance
with an empty body-glob; project-scope sites do not.

* **Secret access readiness**: The CB v2 connection creation LRO itself serves as the propagation test.
  If the Cloud Build Service Agent cannot yet access the secrets (IAM grant not propagated), the LRO fails.
  The caller retries with exponential backoff, deleting the zombie connection resource between attempts.
* **Secret resource propagation**: `getIamPolicy` on a newly created secret may return HTTP 404 while
  the Secret Manager resource propagates across Google's control plane.  The API documents that
  `getIamPolicy` returns an empty policy (HTTP 200) when no policy has been set — so 404 indicates
  the resource itself is not yet visible, not that the policy is absent.  After creating secrets,
  `rbgu_poll_until_ok` polls each secret's `getIamPolicy` endpoint until 200 before proceeding to
  grant IAM bindings.

* **SA keys subresource propagation**: After
  link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/create[serviceAccounts.create]
  returns 200, the keys subresource read path
  (link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/list[serviceAccounts.keys.list])
  can lag the SA email path independently — empirically observed as a non-monotonic 200→200→404 flap where
  both the SA GET probe and a keys subresource `rbgu_poll_until_ok` probe return 200 while the real
  `keys.list` call immediately returns 404 on the same URL.  Mitigation: retry the actual `keys.list`
  call on HTTP 404 using the same backoff budget as step 7 keys.create (`RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_*`) —
  same underlying physics (subresource visibility after parent creation).  A separate
  `rbgu_poll_until_ok` proxy probe before `keys.list` was found insufficient: it returned 200 in the
  same flap window that the real call observed 404, exemplifying why proxy probes are not retained as
  correctness gates (see "What Dies" below).  See evidence row below.

==== Rationale

The connection creation LRO tests the actual operation, not a proxy.
When the Cloud Build Service Agent accesses resources requiring recently-granted permissions,
a successful LRO proves end-to-end readiness: the IAM grant has propagated,
the service agent identity is active, and the resource content is valid.

Fixed `sleep` delays ("sleep and pray") are unreliable:

* Google's IAM propagation documentation states changes "typically take less than 2 minutes"
  but provides no guaranteed upper bound.
* A 15-second sleep is too short for worst-case propagation and wastefully long for the common case.
* Retry-on-error tests the actual operation: if the grant has propagated, the retry succeeds immediately;
  if not, the retry continues until the deadline.

The `testIamPermissions` API cannot serve as a readiness gate because it only tests the caller's own
permissions on a resource -- it cannot test whether a third-party service account (like the CB service agent)
has been granted access to a secret.

==== Superseded: Impersonation Readiness Gate

The prior approach used an impersonation readiness gate: mint a short-lived token as the CB service agent
via `generateAccessToken`, then call `accessSecretVersion` with that impersonated token, retrying on 403
until the grant propagates.  This approach was abandoned due to three problems discovered 2026-03-03:

. **roles/owner does NOT grant iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken** --
  The explicit `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant (previously deleted as unnecessary per
  an earlier RBSCIP revision) was in fact required for impersonation.  Without it,
  `generateAccessToken` returns persistent 403.  roles/owner is a broad role but does not include
  the `iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken` permission needed for impersonation.

. **Wrong SA identity** -- The code impersonated the default build SA
  (`{PN}@cloudbuild.gserviceaccount.com`) but CB v2 connections use the Cloud Build Service Agent
  (`service-{PN}@gcp-sa-cloudbuild.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).  Even if impersonation worked,
  it would have tested the wrong principal.

. **No non-impersonation alternative exists** -- `testIamPermissions` and IAM Policy Analyzer
  reflect policy intent (what the policy document says), not enforcement state (whether the grant
  has actually propagated).  Only actual access as the principal proves propagation.

Pattern B (connection-creation retry) eliminates `generateAccessToken`, `accessSecretVersion`,
and any need for `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grants for the readiness gate.

==== Evidence

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Claim
|Source

|IAM changes propagate eventually, typically < 2 minutes
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/access-change-propagation[Access change propagation] -- "After you grant access, the access change takes effect after a period of time known as propagation delay"

|Newly created SA may not be immediately usable
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-create[Creating service accounts] -- "After you create a service account, you might need to wait 60 seconds or more before you use the service account"

|testIamPermissions tests caller permissions only
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/testing-permissions[Testing permissions] -- tests whether the caller has the specified permissions on a resource

|CB v2 connection creation uses the Cloud Build Service Agent to read secrets
|https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/github/connect-repo-github[Connect a GitHub host] -- the Cloud Build Service Agent must have `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` on the secrets used by the connection

|SA keys subresource read-path can flap non-monotonically (200→200→404) after SA create
|`Memos/memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` -- transcript-level evidence, pristine depot `prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009`, 2026-05-14
|===

==== What Dies

* The impersonation readiness gate (`generateAccessToken` + `accessSecretVersion` as a pre-check)
* Any `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant solely for the readiness gate
  (build-time Mason impersonation grants are unrelated and preserved)
* Fixed `sleep 15` delays before CB v2 connection creation and after Governor SA creation
* Standalone `rbgu_poll_until_ok` as a correctness gate for IAM propagation
  (the SA GET poll remains as a fast-path existence check, not a grant propagation gate)
* Any assumption that SA existence implies SA usability in IAM policy operations

==== What Survives

* SA GET poll via `rbgu_poll_until_ok` -- retained as a fast-path check that the SA resource itself
  is readable, which typically resolves well before IAM policy propagation completes
* The existing 429/5xx transient retry loop in {rbtoe_iam_grant_project} step 3 --
  this handles a different failure class (rate limiting, server errors) and is preserved inside
  the new outer propagation retry
* All IAM grant functions ({rbtoe_iam_grant_project}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
  {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}) -- enhanced with propagation retry,
  not replaced
* Cloud Build service agent `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant on Mason SA --
  this is for build-time impersonation (Cloud Build executing as Mason), unrelated to the readiness gate

==== Backoff Profile

All IAM-grant propagation retries — every site in `rbgi_IAM.sh` plus the
inline GAR retry in `rbgg_invest_director` — use the same single profile
locked in `rbgc_Constants.sh`:

* Initial delay: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC` (3 seconds)
* Multiplier: 2x (per-site arithmetic, not a kindle constant)
* Cap: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC` (20 seconds)
* Deadline: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC` (420 seconds; 7 minutes)

This profile accommodates Google's documented "typically < 2 minutes" with margin for worst-case
propagation while avoiding indefinite waits. It is the single budget shared
across all three propagation classes (A: forward member-visibility, B:
backward member-visibility, C: caller-recently-empowered); operator value
changes propagate to every grant site by editing the constants once. The
JWT-bearer consumer-side retry uses its own budget
(`RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_*`) — different cost-of-waiting profile.

==== References

* Research conducted: 2026-03-03 (API documentation review, live 403 diagnosis)
* Google IAM documentation consistently describes eventual consistency without hard SLA bounds
* Pattern B (connection-creation-as-probe) replaces the original `generateAccessToken` / `accessSecretVersion` gate

content.text:
Read RBSDE-depot_levy.adoc and RBSCIP-IamPropagation.adoc. Three observations and a sharpened repair.

**Finding 1 — spec gap: worker pool create step lacks `{rbbc_await}`.** RBSDE step "Create Private Worker Pool" (lines 81–101) calls `workerPools.create` but only treats 409 as idempotent — no `{rbbc_await} returned operation` clause. Compare:
- "Create Depot Project" (line 41–52) → has `{rbbc_await} returned operation`
- "Create Container Repository" (line 124–153) → has `{rbbc_await} returned operation`
- "Create Private Worker Pool" → does **not**

All three are documented LROs but only worker pool create omits the await. The spec itself encodes the latent bug: there is no documented synchronization barrier between pool creation and any downstream use. Before ₢BOAAD, the first downstream use happened minutes later in a separate operator action (hallmark conjure), so wall-clock latency masked it. The new probe step at line 192 fires immediately in the same script and surfaces it.

**Finding 2 — spec drift: singular pool described, dual pools created.** RBSDE "Create Private Worker Pool" (line 81–101) describes a singular `WORKER_POOL_STEM`. The implementation has been dual-pool (`STEM+tether`, `STEM+airgap`) for a while via `RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`. The new "Submit Per-Pool Probe Builds" step (line 192+, this morning's ₢BOAAD work) is dual-pool aware and iterates over `[tether, airgap]`. So the create step is the spec drift, not the probe step. This is independent of the failure but lives in the same axhos block, so any fix touching the create step should fold both together.

**Finding 3 — RBSCIP gives the repair philosophy and budget, but the choice between two patterns is not pre-decided for LRO readiness.** RBSCIP Pattern B is explicit: "test the actual operation, not a proxy." Backoff profile is shared and locked: 3s initial, 2x, 20s cap, 420s deadline. But RBSCIP's evidence rows are all IAM-propagation cases (member visibility, caller-recently-empowered, secret resource). LRO-completion-readiness for a freshly created GCP resource isn't covered there — it's a different physics.

For LRO readiness the project already has a different pattern: `{rbbc_await} returned operation`. That's the *spec-level* idiom for "this is an LRO; gate the next step on it finishing." It's used at project create and repository create.

**The two repair shapes RBSCIP and RBSDE jointly suggest:**

- **(A) `{rbbc_await}` on pool create.** Add the await clause to RBSDE's "Create Private Worker Pool" step (closing Finding 1's spec gap), and implement it in `rbgp_Payor.sh:904/930` by extracting `.name` from the create response and polling the operations endpoint until `.done == true`. Matches the existing project-create / repo-create idiom. Doesn't open a new pattern.
- **(B) Retry the probe submission.** Treat "workerpool not found" as a propagation-class transient and wrap probe submission in RBSCIP-style backoff retry. Matches RBSCIP "test the actual operation." But this stretches RBSCIP's domain (it's about IAM, not LRO completion), and submitting a build per attempt has side effects RBSCIP retries don't — each accepted submit is a real build that consumes quota.

**(A) is the right shape.** It closes a real spec gap rather than introducing a new pattern; it composes cleanly with the existing dual-pool drift fix in the same axhos step; it has zero side effects per attempt (operations.get is read-only); and the rule "if a GCP create call returns an Operation, you must await it before using the resource" is a property of GCP, not of this codebase — encoding it in the spec is the correct level.

**Plan for the repair:**
1. RBSDE edit: convert "Create Private Worker Pool" to dual-pool shape (matching the probe step's `[tether, airgap]` iteration) and add `{rbbc_await} returned operation` after each create.
2. `rbgp_Payor.sh` edit: after each pool create's 200/201 branch at lines 907 and 933, capture `.name` from the response and poll `operations.get` until `.done == true` using the existing `rbgu_poll_until_ok`-shape harness (or a thin wrapper that checks `.done` rather than HTTP code).

Want me to proceed?

content.text:
Yes, plausibly. Here's the reasoning, and a verification step worth doing before we ship the repair.

**Why it's plausible.** `workerPools.create` is an LRO. The 200/201 you see at submission only acknowledges the *create request* — the actual pool gets provisioned asynchronously. From the levy log timing:

- 15:49:03 — "Create dual worker pools" step begins
- 15:49:05 — proceeds to next step (2 seconds later; that's just the API accepting both create POSTs)
- 15:49:49 — first probe submission, 46 seconds after create — fails "workerpool not found"

If the e2-highcpu-32 machine type can't be allocated (quota not granted), the create LRO terminates in FAILED state and the pool never materializes. From the probe's perspective at 15:49:49, "workerpool not found" is indistinguishable between:

- (a) **Async-readiness**: pool LRO still in progress, will eventually succeed
- (b) **Quota terminally failed**: LRO terminated with an error, pool will never exist

46s is on the short end for worker pool provisioning, so (a) was already plausible. But (b) is equally consistent with the symptom, and the commit message `ba3b7b3d` literally flags it: *"quota check advised before."* If the gauntlet ran without that check completing, the bump itself is a sufficient cause.

**Does this change the repair?** No — `{rbbc_await} returned operation` fixes both cases. The difference is the operator ergonomics:

- Under (a), the await loop succeeds after ~1–3 minutes and the levy proceeds.
- Under (b), the await loop surfaces the LRO's terminal error (e.g., "quota exceeded for `CPUS` in region us-central1") **at the create step**, not as "workerpool not found" five steps later. The actual root cause becomes legible.

So the repair direction is unchanged, but the urgency of doing it cleanly goes up — without it, the e2-highcpu-32 trial that's already merged on the regime will keep producing this same misleading "workerpool not found" symptom every time the pool's LRO terminally fails.

**Verification step worth doing now (cheap):** the depot project `prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100011` was created at 15:48:19 and the levy errored out before completion — the project (and the failed pool LRO state) may still exist. We can confirm which case happened this morning by:

1. `gcloud builds worker-pools list --project=prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100011 --region=us-central1` — does the pool exist, and what's its `state`?
2. If it exists but in a non-`RUNNING` state, the LRO terminal status will name the cause directly.

If the answer comes back "quota exceeded for e2-highcpu-32 CPUs," we have two scoped follow-ups: the await repair (₢BOAAA covers this), and a separate question for ₣BL/the machine-type trial about whether to request quota or revert. If the pool is `RUNNING`, we're in case (a) and it was async readiness as originally diagnosed.

message.content: What was stored in the temp dir that you were looking for?  the tP tabtarget uses BUK logging which is not the unix temp location

content.text:
You're right — I was confusing the durable BUK logs (`../logs-buk/`, which we still have) with the per-invocation `BURD_TEMP_DIR` scratch that gets sandboxed under the theurge trace dir during gauntlet runs.

What I was after specifically: the response body capture from the `depot_pool_tether_create` POST — i.e., the JSON the `workerPools.create` call returned. `rbgu_http_json` writes those to its temp dir as scratch (request body, response body, response headers, response code per call). When the workbench runs standalone they're under `../temp-buk/temp-NNN/`; when it runs *under theurge* during the gauntlet, theurge sandboxes the workbench's temp inside the trace dir at `/tmp/rbtd-NNN/burv/invoke-NN/temp/`.

What I wanted from that response body: the LRO `Operation.name` from `workerPools.create`. With that, `operations.get` would tell me directly whether the create operation reached terminal state and what error (if any) it carried — answering case (a) vs (b) without needing gcloud at all.

But those captures are scratch by design — they're transient working files, not durable evidence. The durable trail is `../logs-buk/` (which has step-level `buc_step` / `buc_info` output but not raw API response bodies). So once the trace dir is gone, that path is closed and the only way to learn the pool's actual terminal state is to ask GCP directly via gcloud or the console.

Which is what you've already redirected me toward. Standing by for the `gcloud auth login` output (or for you to point me at the console-tab evidence).

content.text:
Agreed — theurge sandboxing the workbench inside /tmp is a real bug, not just an inconvenience. Here's the full list this conversation surfaced.

## Code defects

1. **`workerPools.create` LRO not awaited** (`rbgp_Payor.sh:887–937`). The HTTP 200/201 acknowledges create acceptance, not provisioning success. The new probe submission at lines 1098–1099 fires immediately, so a pool that's still provisioning — or has terminally failed its LRO — surfaces as "workerpool not found" at probe time rather than as a clean failure at the create step. This is the proximate cause of the gauntlet failure.

2. **Theurge bypasses BUK temp/log convention.** Theurge places its trace dir at `/tmp/rbtd-NNN/` and sandboxes the workbench's `BURD_TEMP_DIR` and output dir inside it (`/tmp/rbtd-NNN/burv/invoke-NN/temp|output`). Standalone workbench invocations use `../temp-buk/` and `../output-buk/`, which survived the reboot; theurge-sandboxed artifacts did not. Per-call HTTP captures from a failing gauntlet run vanished with /tmp. Theurge should write under the same BUK roots so forensic evidence persists across reboots and tmpfiles cleanups.

## RBS*.adoc updates that were neglected

3. **RBSDE-depot_levy "Create Private Worker Pool" missing `{rbbc_await} returned operation`** (lines 81–101). Compare "Create Depot Project" (lines 41–52) and "Create Container Repository" (lines 124–153) — both LROs, both have the await clause. Worker pool create is the only LRO without it. The spec itself encodes the bug from defect #1.

4. **RBSDE-depot_levy "Create Private Worker Pool" describes single pool, implementation creates two.** Spec says `workerPoolId: «WORKER_POOL_STEM»` (singular). Implementation creates `STEM+tether` and `STEM+airgap` via `RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`. The newer "Submit Per-Pool Probe Builds" step (line 192+, added by ₢BOAAD this morning) iterates `[tether, airgap]` — so the spec is internally inconsistent: the probe step references dual pools that the create step never documents creating.

## Regime-level concerns

5. **e2-highcpu-32 machine type without confirmed quota** (commit `ba3b7b3d`, ₣BL). The bump commit itself flags "quota check advised before." If quota isn't in place, the worker pool LRO terminally fails — the await fix in defect #1 would surface this clearly ("quota exceeded for CPUS") rather than as the misleading "workerpool not found" five steps later, but quota is still an operator decision (request increase, or revert to a quota-safe machine type). Live verification against `prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100011` would tell us whether this morning's gauntlet failure was case (a) async readiness or case (b) terminally-failed quota — currently blocked on Payor authentication.

## Already-captured this morning (for completeness, not new finds)

6. Notebook-output commit size pressure (tripped 30KB warning twice during ₢BOAAC).
7. No automated regression coverage for the notebook→Anthropic round trip.

Items 1, 3, and 4 cluster on the same axhos step in RBSDE and the same call site in `rbgp_Payor.sh` — natural to land as a single pace under ₢BOAAA. Item 2 is independent and shapes new pace shape. Item 5 is operator-decision; item 6/7 already live as itches.

**Cluster A — issues 1 + 3 + 4 (LRO await + RBSDE spec gap + RBSDE single→dual drift)**

*Confidence: medium.* The diagnosis and the shape ("add await; convert spec step to dual-pool; poll operations.get until `.done == true`") are clear. What I haven't verified:

- How project-create and repo-create *actually* implement their `{rbbc_await}` clauses in code. The spec specifies the contract but I'd want to read the existing implementations and follow the established convention rather than invent one. Specifically: do they have a dedicated `rbgu_await_operation` helper, or is the await inlined? Does `rbgu_poll_until_ok` already cover this with `.done`-aware predicate, or do I need a sibling helper that distinguishes "operation done, error empty" from "operation done, error populated"?
- The backoff budget. RBSCIP locks 3s/2x/20s-cap/420s-deadline for IAM propagation, but worker pool provisioning is a different physics — cold pool creates can take ~5 minutes. Whether to reuse the RBSCIP budget, define a new constant family, or use an operation-specific bound is a regime-constants decision I haven't seen made elsewhere.
- The terminal-error path: when the operation completes with `.done == true` but `.error` is populated (case b), the levy should `buc_die` with that error. Wording / format matches existing patterns I haven't surveyed.

Slatable once I do a focused read of `rbgp_Payor.sh` project/repo-create implementations and the `rbgu_*` LRO helpers (if any). Maybe 15 minutes of code reading. Until then I'd be guessing at the convention.

Theurge is Rust, not bash — I'd be reading new code. The docket character is design-conversation, not mechanical. I'd slate this as a `stabled`-class shape pace first (capture the bug and intended-state in the docket, defer implementation) rather than `racing` it now.

# jjezs_slate levy-pool-lro-await

## Character
Mechanical — the existing `{rbbc_await} returned operation` pattern in
RBSDE-depot_levy (used at "Create Depot Project" and "Create Container
Repository") is the template. Match it.

## Goal
Make `workerPools.create` await its LRO terminal state before any
downstream code uses the pool. Surface terminal errors (e.g., machine-type
quota denial) at the create step rather than as a misleading "workerpool
not found" five steps later at the new probe-submission step.

## Boundary
- Code: `rbgp_Payor.sh` pool-create call sites — match the existing
  project-create / repo-create LRO handling in the same module.
- Spec: RBSDE-depot_levy "Create Private Worker Pool" step gains
  `{rbbc_await} returned operation`, matching project-create and
  repo-create steps in the same spec.
- Spec drift in the same step: convert singular `WORKER_POOL_STEM`
  description to dual `[tether, airgap]` iteration matching the existing
  "Submit Per-Pool Probe Builds" step.

## Done
- pristine-lifecycle fixture's `depot_stand_up` case clears the
